verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Peter saw it, he answered unto the people, Ye men of Israel, why marvel ye at this, or why look ye so earnestly on us, as though by our own power or holiness we have made this man to walk?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is greater freedom of speech in this harangue, than in the former.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not that he was afraid on the former occasion, but the persons whom he addressed there, being jesters and scoffers, would not have borne it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hence in the beginning of that address he also bespeaks their attention by his preamble; “Be this known unto you, and hearken to my words.” (ch. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But here there is no need of this management. (κατασκευἥς.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For his hearers were not in a state of indifference.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The miracle had aroused them all; they were even full of fear and amazement.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore also there was no need of beginning at that point, but rather with a different topic; by which, in fact, he powerfully conciliated them, namely, by rejecting the glory which was to be had from them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For nothing is so advantageous, and so likely to pacify the hearers, as to say nothing about one’s self of an honorable nature, but, on the contrary, to obviate all surmise of wishing to do so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, in truth, much more did they increase their glory by despising glory, and showing that what had just taken place was no human act, but a Divine work; and that it was their part to join with the beholders in admiration, rather than to receive it from them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see how clear of all ambition he is, and how he repels the honor paid to him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the same manner also did the ancient fathers; for instance, Daniel said, “Not for any wisdom that is in me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again Joseph, “Do not interpretations belong to God?”

verses.text: And David, “When the lion and the bear came, in the name of the Lord I rent them with my hands.”

verses.text: And so likewise here the Apostles, “Why look ye so earnestly on us, as though by our own power or holiness we had made this man to walk?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, not even this; for not by our own merit did we draw down the Divine influence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The God of Abraham, and of Isaac, and of Jacob, the God of our fathers.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how assiduously he thrusts himself (εἰσωθει) upon the fathers of old, lest he should appear to be introducing a new doctrine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the former address he appealed to the patriarch David, here he appeals to Abraham and the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Hath glorified His Servant Jesus.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again a lowly expression, like as in the opening address.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But at this point he proceeds to enlarge upon the outrage, and exalts the heinousness of the deed, no longer, as before, throwing a veil over it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This he does, wishing to work upon them more powerfully.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the more he proved them accountable, the better his purpose were effected.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Hath glorified,” he says, “His Servant Jesus, Whom ye delivered up, and denied Him in the presence of Pilate, when he was determined to let him go.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The charge is twofold: Pilate was desirous to let Him go; you would not, when he was willing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But ye denied the Holy One and the Just, and desired a murderer to be granted unto you; and killed the Prince (or Author) of Life: Whom God hath raised from the dead; whereof we are witnesses.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye desired a robber instead of Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows the great aggravation of the act.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As he has them under his hand, he now strikes hard.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The Prince of Life,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In these words he establishes the doctrine of the Resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whom God hath raised from the dead.” (ch. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whence doth this appear?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He no longer refers to the Prophets, but to himself, inasmuch as now he has a right to be believed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Before, when he affirmed that He was risen, he adduced the testimony of David; now, having said it, he alleges the College of Apostles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whereof we are witnesses,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And His name, through faith in His name, hath made this man strong, whom ye see and know: yea, the faith which is by Him hath given him this perfect soundness in the presence of you all.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seeking to declare the matter (ζητὥν τὸ πρἅγμα εὶπεἵν), he straightway brings forward the sign: “In the presence,” he says, “of you all.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As he had borne hard upon them, and had shown that He Whom they crucified had risen, again he relaxes, by giving them the power of repentance; “And now, brethren, I wot that through ignorance ye did it, as did also your rulers.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is one ground of excuse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The second is of a different kind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As Joseph speaks to his brethren, “God did send me before you (Gen. xlv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5); what in the former speech he had briefly said, in the words, “Him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have taken,”—this he here enlarges upon: “But what God before had showed by the mouth of all His Prophets, that His Christ should suffer, He hath so fulfilled.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the same time showing, that it was not of their doing, if this be proved, that it took place after God’s counsel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He alludes to those words with which they had reviled Him on the Cross, namely “Let Him deliver Him, if He will have Him; for He said, I am the Son of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If He trust in God, let Him now come down from the cross.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O foolish men, were these idle words?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It must needs so come to pass, and the prophets bear witness thereunto.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore if He descended not, it was for no weakness of His own that He did not come down, but for very power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Peter puts this by way of apology for the Jews, hoping that they may also close with what he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He hath so fulfilled,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see now how he refers everything to that source?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Repent ye therefore,” he says, “and be converted.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not add, “from your sins;” but, “that your sins, may be blotted out,” means the same thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then he adds the gain: “So shall the times of refreshing come from the presence of the Lord.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This betokens them in a sad state, brought low by many wars.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is to the case of one on fire, and craving comfort, that the expression applies.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see now how he advances.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In his first sermon, he but slightly hinted at the resurrection, and Christ’s sitting in heaven; but here he also speaks of His visible advent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And He shall send Jesus the Christ ordained (for you), “Whom the heaven must (i.e. must of necessity) receive, until the times of the restitution of all things.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The reason why He does not now come is clear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which God hath spoken,” he continues, “by the mouth of His holy prophets since the world began.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For Moses truly said unto the fathers, A Prophet shall the Lord your God raise up unto you of your brethren, like unto me; him shall ye hear in all things whatsoever he shall say unto you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Before, he had spoken of David, here he speaks of Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Of all things,” he says, “which He hath spoken.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he does not say, “which Christ,” but, “which God hath spoken by the mouth of all His holy prophets since the world began.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he betakes him to the ground of credibility, saying, “A Prophet shall the Lord your God raise up unto you of your brethren, like unto me; Him shall ye hear in all things.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then the greatness of the punishment: “And it shall come to pass, that every soul which will not hear that Prophet, shall be destroyed from among the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yea, and all the prophets, from Samuel and those that follow, after, as many as have spoken have likewise foretold of these days.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He has done well to set the distinction here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For whenever he says anything great, he appeals to them of old.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he found a text which contained both truths; just as in the other discourse he said, “Until He put His foes under His feet.” (ch. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The remarkable circumstance is, that the two things stand together; that is, subjection and disobedience, and the punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Like unto me,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then why are ye alarmed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye are the children of the prophets” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25): so that to you they spake, and for your sakes have all these things come to pass.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as they deemed that through their outrage they had become alienated (and indeed there is no parity of reason, that He Who now is crucified, should now cherish them as His own), he proves to them that both the one and the other are in accordance with prophecy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye are the children,” he says, “of the Prophets, and of the covenant which God made with our fathers, saying unto Abraham, ‘And in thy seed shall all the kindreds of the earth be blessed.’

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Unto you first,” he continues, “God having raised up His Son (τόν Παἵδα) sent Him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To others indeed also, but to you first who crucified Him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To bless you,” he adds, “in turning away every one of you from his iniquities.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now let us consider again more minutely what has been read out.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the first place, he establishes the point that the miracle was performed by them; saying, “Why marvel ye?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he will not let the assertion be disbelieved: and to give it more weight, he anticipates their judgment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Why look ye,” he says, “so earnestly on us, as though by our own power or holiness we had made this man to walk?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If this troubles and confounds you, learn Who was the Doer, and be not amazed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how on all occasions when he refers to God, and says that all things are from Him, then he fearlessly chides them: as above where he said, “A man approved of God among you.” (ch. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And on all occasions he reminds them of the outrage they had committed, in order that the fact of the Resurrection may be established.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But here he also subjoins something else; for he no more says, “of Nazareth,” but what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The God of our fathers hath glorified His Servant Jesus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe also the modesty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He reproached them not, neither did he say at once, “Believe then now: behold, a man that has been forty years lame, has been raised up through the name of Jesus Christ.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This he did not say, for it would have excited opposition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On the contrary, he begins by commending them for admiring the deed, and again calls them after their ancestor: “Ye men of Israel.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, he does not say, It was Jesus that healed him: but, “The God of our fathers hath glorified,” etc. But then, lest they should say, How can this stand to reason—that God should glorify the transgressor? therefore he reminds them of the judgment before Pilate, showing that, would they but consider, He was no transgressor; else Pilate had not wished to release Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, “when Pilate was desirous,” but, “was determined to let Him go.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But ye denied the Holy One,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Him who had killed others, ye asked to be released; Him Who quickeneth them that are killed, ye did not wish to have!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that they might not ask again, How should it be that God now glorifies Him, when before He gave no assistance? he brings forward the prophets, testifying that so it behooved to be.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But those things which God before had showed,” etc., (infra v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, lest they should suppose that God’s dispensation was their own apology, first he reproves them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, that the denying Him “to Pilate’s face,” was no ordinary thing; seeing that he wished to release Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that ye cannot deny this, the man who was asked in preference to Him is witness against you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This also is part of a deep dispensation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here it shows their shamelessness and effrontery; that a Gentile, one who saw Him for the first time, should have discharged Him, though he had heard nothing striking; while they who had been brought up among His miracles, have done the very opposite!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, as he has said, “When he (Pilate) had determined to let Him go,” that it may not be imagined that he did this of favor, we read, “And he said, It is a custom with you to release one prisoner: will ye therefore that I release unto you this man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But ye denied the Holy One and the Just.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say, “Ye delivered up;” but everywhere, “Ye denied.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, said they, “We have no king but Cæsar.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say only, Ye did not beg off the innocent, and, “Ye denied” Him but, “Ye slew” Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: While they were hardened, he refrained from such language; but when their minds are most moved, then he strikes home, now that they are in a condition to feel it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For just as when men are drunk we say nothing to them, but when they are sober, and are recovered from their intoxication then we chide them; thus did Peter: when they were able to understand his words, then he also sharpened his tongue, alleging against them many charges; that, Whom God had glorified, they had delivered up; Whom Pilate would have acquitted they denied to his face; that they preferred the robber before Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe again how he speaks covertly concerning Christ’s power, showing that He raised Himself: just as in his first discourse he had said, “Because it was not possible that He should be holden of it” (ch. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24), so here he says, “And killed the Prince of Life.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It follows that the Life He had was not from another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The prince (or author) of evil would be he that first brought forth evil; the prince or author of murder, he who first originated murder; so also the Prince (or Author) of Life must be He Who has Life from Himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whom God raised up,” he continues: and now that he has uttered this, he adds, “And his name, upon faith in his name, hath made this man strong, whom ye see and know; yea, the faith which is by Him hath given Him this perfect soundness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: [The faith which is by Him ἡ δι᾽ αὐτοὕ πίστις.]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet it was ἡ εἰς αὐτὸν πιστις, “the faith which is in Him” (as its object) that did all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the Apostles did not say, “By the name,” but, “In the name,” and it was in Him (εἰς αὐτὸν) that the man believed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they did not yet make bold to use the expression, “The faith which is in Him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, that the phrase “By Him” should not be too low, observe that after saying, “Upon the faith of His name,” he adds, “His name hath made him strong,” and then it is that he says, “Yea, the faith which is by Him hath given him this perfect soundness.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he implies, that in the καὶ ἐκεἵνο former expression also “Whom God raised up,” he did but condescend to their low attainments.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that Person needed not Another’s help for His rising again, Whose Name raised up a lame man, being all one as dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how on all occasions he adduces their own testimony.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus above, he said, “As ye yourselves also know;” and, “In the midst of you:” and here again, “Whom ye see and know: in the presence of you all.” (ch. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet that it was, “In His name,” they knew not: but they did know that the man was lame, that he stands there whole.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They that had wrought the deed themselves confessed, that it was not by their own power, but by that of Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And had this assertion been unfounded, had they not been truly persuaded themselves that Christ had risen again, they would not have sought to establish the honor of a dead man instead of their own, especially while the eyes of the multitude were upon them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, when their minds were alarmed, immediately he encourages them, by the appellation of Brethren, “And now, brethren, I wot, etc.” For in the former discourse he foretold nothing, but only says concerning Christ, “Therefore let all the house of Israel know assuredly:” here he adds an admonition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There he waited till the people spoke: here, he knew how much they had already effected, and that the present assembly was better disposed toward them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “That through ignorance ye did it.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet the circumstances mentioned above were not to be put to the score of ignorance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To choose the robber, to reject Him Who had been adjudged to be acquitted, to desire even to destroy Him—how should this be referred to ignorance?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nevertheless, he gives them liberty to deny it, and to change their mind about what had happened.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now this indeed, that you put to death the innocent, ye knew: but that you were killing “the Prince of Life,” this, belike, ye did not know.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he exculpated not them alone, but also the chief contrivers of the evil, “ye and your rulers:” for doubtless it would have roused their opposition, had he gone off into accusation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the evil-doer, when you accuse him of some wickedness that he has done, in his endeavor to exonerate himself, grows more vehement.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he no longer says, “Ye crucified,” “Ye killed,” but, “Ye did it;” leading them to seek for pardon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If those rulers did it through ignorance, much more did these present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But these things which God before had showed,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it is remarkable, that both in the first and in the second discourse, speaking to the same effect, that is, in the former, “By the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God;” and in this, “God before had showed that Christ should suffer;” in neither does he adduce any particular text in proof.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The fact is, that each one of such passages is accompanied with many accusations, and with mention of the punishment in store for them [as]; “I will deliver up,” says one, “the wicked in requital for His grave, and the rich in return for His death.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again, * * * “Those things,” he says, “which God before had showed by the mouth of all His prophets, that Christ should suffer, He hath so fulfilled.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows the greatness of that “counsel,” in that all spoke of it, and not one only.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It does not follow, because the event was through ignorance, that it took place irrespectively of God’s ordinance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how great is the Wisdom of God, when it uses the wickedness of others to bring about that which must be.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He hath fulfilled,” he says: that they may not imagine that anything at all is wanting; for whatsoever Christ must needs suffer, has been fulfilled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But do not think, that, because the Prophets said this, and because ye did it through ignorance, this sufficeth to your exculpation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, he does not express himself thus, but in milder terms says, “Repent ye therefore.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For either it was through ignorance, or by the dispensation of God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “That your sins may be blotted out.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I do not mean the crimes committed at the Crucifixion; perhaps they were through ignorance; but so that your other sins may be blotted out: this only.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “So shall the times of refreshing come unto you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he speaks of the Resurrection, obscurely.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For those are indeed times of refreshing, which Paul also looked for, when he said, “We that are in this tabernacle do groan, being burthened.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then to prove that Christ is the cause of the days of refreshing, he says, “And He shall send Jesus Christ, which before was for you ordained.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He said not, “That your sin may be blotted out,” but, “your sins;” for he hints at that sin also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He shall send.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whom the heaven must receive.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Still [“must”] “receive?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why not simply, Whom the heaven hath received?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This, as if discoursing of old times: so, he says, it is divinely ordered, so it is settled: not a word yet of His eternal subsistence.—“For Moses indeed said unto the fathers, A Prophet shall the Lord raise up for you:” “Him shall ye hear in all things that He shall speak unto you:” and having said, “All things which God hath spoken by the mouth of all His holy Prophets,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22) now indeed he brings in Christ Himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, if He predicted many things and it is necessary to hear Him, one would not be wrong in saying that the Prophets have spoken these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, besides, he wishes to show that the Prophets did predict the same things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, if any one will look closely into the matter, he will find these things spoken in the Old Testament, obscurely indeed, but nevertheless spoken.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who was purposely designed,” says he: in Whom there is nothing novel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he also alarms them, by the thought that much remains to be fulfilled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if so, how says he, “Hath fulfilled?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The things which it was necessary “that Christ should suffer,” are fulfilled: the things which must come to pass, not yet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “A prophet shall the Lord God raise up for you from among your brethren, like unto me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This would most conciliate them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe the sprinkling of low matters and high, side by side,—that He Who was to go up into the heavens should be like unto Moses?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet it was a great thing too.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in fact He was not simply like unto Moses, if so be that “every soul which will not hear shall be destroyed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And one might mention numberless other things which show that He was not like unto Moses; so that it is a mighty text that he has handled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “God shall raise Him up unto you,” says Moses, “from among your brethren,” etc.: consequently Moses himself threatens those that should not hear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yea, and all the prophets,” etc.: all this is calculated to attract “Yea, and all the prophets,” says the Apostle, “from Samuel.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He refrains from enumerating them singly, not to make his discourse too long; but having alleged that decisive testimony of Moses, he passes by the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye,” he says, “are the children of the Prophets, and of the covenant which God made.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25) “Children of the covenant;” that is, heirs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For lest they should think that they received this offer from the favor of Peter, he shows, that of old it was due to them, in order that they may the rather believe that such also is the will of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Unto you first,” he continues, “God having raised up His Son Jesus, sent Him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say simply, “Unto you He sent His Son,” but also, after the resurrection, and when He had been crucified.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that they may not suppose that he himself granted them this favor, and not the Father, he says, “To bless you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if He is your Brother, and blesses you, the affair is a promise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Unto you first.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That is, so far are you from having no share in these blessings, that He would have you become moreover promoters and authors of them to others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For you are not to feel like castaways.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Having raised up”: again, the Resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In turning away,” he says, “every one of you from his iniquities.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In this way He blesses you: not in a general way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what kind of blessing is this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A great one.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of course not the turning a man away from his iniquities is itself sufficient to remit them also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if it is not sufficient to remit, how should it be to confer a blessing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is not to be supposed that the transgressor becomes forthwith also blessed; he is simply released from his sins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this, “Like unto me,” would no wise apply.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Hear ye Him,” he says; and not this alone, but he adds, “And it shall come to pass, that every soul, which will not hear that Prophet, shall be destroyed from among the people.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When he has shown them that they had sinned, and has imparted forgiveness to them, and promised good things, then indeed, then he says, “Moses also says the same thing.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What sort of connection is this: “Until the times of the restitution;” and then to introduce Moses, saying, that all that Christ said shall come to pass?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then also, on the other hand, he says, as matter of encomium (so that for this reason also ye ought to obey): “Ye are the children of the prophets and of the covenant:” i.e. heirs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then why do you stand affected towards that which is your own, as if it were another’s?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, you have done deeds worthy of condemnation; still you may yet obtain pardon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having said this, with reason he is now able to say, “Unto you God sent his Son Jesus to bless you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He says not, To save you, but what is greater; that the crucified Jesus blessed His crucifiers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then also imitate Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us cast out that spirit of murder and enmity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not enough not to retaliate (for even in the Old Dispensation this was exemplified); but let us do all as we would for bosom-friends, as we would for ourselves so for those who have injured us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We are followers of Him, we are His disciples, who after being crucified, sets everything in action in behalf of his murderers, and sends out His Apostles to this end.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet we have often suffered justly; but those acted not only unjustly, but impiously; for He was their Benefactor, He had done no evil, and they crucified Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And for what reason?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the sake of their reputation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But He Himself made them objects of reverence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The scribes and the pharisees sit in Moses’ seat; all therefore whatsoever they bid you observe, that do ye, but after their works do ye not.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again in another place, “Go thy way, show thyself to the priest.” (ib. viii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Besides, when He might have destroyed them, He saves them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then imitate Him, and let no one be an enemy, no one a foe, except to the devil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not a little does the habit of not swearing contribute to this end: I mean to the not giving way to wrath: and by not giving way to wrath, we shall not have an enemy either.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lop off the oaths of a man, and you have clipt the wings of his anger, you have smothered all his passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Swearing, it is said, is as the wind to wrath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lower the sails; no need of sails, when there is no wind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then we do not clamor, and do not swear, we have cut the sinews of passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if you doubt this, just put it to experiment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Impose it as a law upon the passionate man that he shall never swear, and you will have no necessity of preaching moderation to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So the whole business is finished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even though you do not forswear yourselves [yet], by swearing at all, do you not know in what absurd consequences you involve yourselves—binding yourselves to an absolute necessity and as with a cord, and putting yourselves to all manner of shifts, as men studying how to rescue their soul from an evil which there is no escaping, or, failing of that, obliged [by that self-imposed necessity] to spend your life thenceforth in vexation, in quarrels, and to curse your wrath?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But all is in vain, and to no purpose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Threaten, be peremptory (διόρισαι), do all, whatever it be, without swearing; [so]: it is in your power to reverse (ἀναλὕσαι) both what you have said and what you have done if you have the mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus on the present day I must needs speak more gently to you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For since ye have heard me, and the greater part of the reformation is achieved by you, now then let us see for what purpose the taking of oaths was introduced, and why allowed to be.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In relating to you their first origin, and when they were conceived, and how, and by whom we shall give you this account in requital for your obedience.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is fit that he who has made his practice right, should be taught the philosophy of the matter, but he who is not yet doing the right, is not worthy to be told the history.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They made many covenants in Abraham’s time, and slew victims, and offered sacrifices, and as yet oaths were not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence then did they come in?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When evil increased, when all was confusion, upside down, when men had turned aside to idolatry: then it was, then, when men appeared no longer worthy to be believed, that they called God as witness, as if thereby giving an adequate surety for what they said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such in fact is the Oath: it is a security where men’s principles cannot be trusted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that in the indictment of the swearer the first charge is this,—that he is not to be trusted without a surety, and a great surety too: for such is the exceeding faithlessness, that they ask not man as surety, but will needs have God!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Secondly, the same charge lies against him who receives the oath: that, in a question of compact, he must drag in God for warranty, and refuse to be satisfied unless he get Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O the excessive stupidity, the insolence of such conduct!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou, a worm, earth and dust, and ashes, and vapor, to drag in thy Lord as the surety, and to compel the other to drag Him in likewise!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell me, if your servants were disputing with each other, and exchanging assurances with each other, and the fellow-servant should declare that for his part he would not be satisfied till he had their common master given him for surety, would he not have stripes given him without number, and be made to know that the master is for other purposes, and not to be put to any such use as this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do I speak of a fellow-servant?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For should he choose any respectable person, would not that person consider it an affront?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But I do not wish to do this, say you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well: then do not compel the other to do so either: since where men only are in question, this is done—if your party says, “I give such an one as my surety,” you do not allow him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What then,” say you, “am I to lose what I have given?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I am not speaking of this; but that you allow him to insult God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For which reason greater shall be the inevitable punishment to him who forces the oath upon another, than to him who takes it: the same holds with regard to him who gives an oath when no one asks him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what makes it worse, is, that every one is ready to swear, for one farthing, for some petty item, for his own injustice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All this may be said, when there is no perjury; but if perjury follow in the train, both he that imposes and he that takes the oath have turned everything upside down.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But there are some things,” you will say, “which are unknown.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well take these into account, and do nothing negligently; but, if you do act negligently, take the loss to yourself as your punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is better to be the loser thus, than in a very different way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For tell me—you force a man to take an oath, with what expectation?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That he will forswear himself?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this is utter insanity; and the judgment will fall upon your own head; better you should lose your money, than he be lost.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why act thus to your own detriment, and to the insulting of God?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the spirit of a wild beast, and of an impious man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But you do this in the expectation that he will not forswear himself?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then trust him without the oath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Nay, there are many,” you reply, “who in the absence of an oath would presume to defraud; but, once the oath taken, would refrain.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You deceive yourself, man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A man having once learnt to steal, and to wrong his neighbor, will presume full oft to trample upon his oath; if on the contrary he shrinks from swearing, he will much more shrink from injustice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he is influenced against his will.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, he deserves pardon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why am I speaking of this kind of oaths, while I pass over those in the market-place?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as regards these last, you can urge none of these pleas.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For ten farthings you there have swearing and forswearing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, because the thunderbolt does not actually fall from heaven, because all things are not overthrown, you stand holding God in your bonds: to get a few vegetables, a pair of shoes, for a little matter of money, calling Him to witness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is the meaning of this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not let us imagine, that because we are not punished, therefore we do not sin; this comes of God’s mercy; not of our merit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let your oath be an imprecation upon your own child, upon your own self: say, “Else let the hangman lash my ribs.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But you dare not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is God less valuable than thy ribs? is He less precious than thy pate?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say “Else let me be struck blind.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Christ so spares us, that He will not let us swear even by our own head; and yet we so little spare the honor of God, that on all occasions we must drag Him in!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye know not what God is, and with what sort of lips he behooves to be invoked.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, when we speak of any man of eminent worth, we say, “First wash your mouth, and then make mention of him:” and yet, that precious Name which is above every name, the Name which is marvellous in all the earth, the Name which devils hear and tremble, we haul about as we list!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Oh! the force of habit! thereby has that Name become cheap.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No doubt, if you impose on any one the necessity of coming into the sacred edifice to take his oath there, you feel that you have made the oath an awful one.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet how is it that it seems awful in this way, but because we have been in the habit of using that at random, but not this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For ought not a shudder of awe to be felt when God is but named?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now, whereas among the Jews His Name was held to be so reverend, that it was written upon plates, and none was allowed to wear the characters except the high-priest alone: we bandy about His Name like any ordinary word.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If simply to name God was not allowed to all; to call Him to witness, what audacity is it! nay, what madness!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if need were (rather than this) to fling away all that you have, ought you not readily to part with all?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold, I solemnly declare and testify; reform these oaths of the forum, these superfluous oaths, and bring to me all those who wish to take them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold, in the presence of this assembly, I charge those who are set apart for the tending of the Houses of Prayer, I exhort and issue this order to them, that no person be allowed to take such oaths at his own discretion: or rather, that none be allowed to swear in any other way, but that the person be brought to me, whosoever he be, since even for these matters less will not serve but they must needs come before us, just as if one had to do with little children.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: May there be no occasion!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a shame in some things still to need to be taught.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you dare to touch the Holy Table, being a person unbaptized?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, but what is still worse, you the baptized dare to lay your hand upon the Holy Table, which not even all ordained persons are allowed to touch, and so to take your oath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now you would not go and lay your hand upon the head of your child, and yet do you touch the Table, and not shudder, not feel afraid?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Bring these men to me; I will judge, and send them away rejoicing, both the one and the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do what you choose; I lay it down as a law that there be no swearing at all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What hope of salvation, while we thus make all to have been done in vain?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is this the end of your bills, and your bonds, that you should sacrifice your own soul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What gain do you get so great as the loss?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Has he forsworn himself?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You have undone both him and yourself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But has he not? even so still you have undone (both), by forcing him to transgress the commandment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us cast out this disease from the soul: at any rate let us drive it out of the forum, out of our shops, out of our other work-places; our profits will but be the greater.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not imagine that the success of your worldly plans is to be ensured by transgressions of the Divine laws.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he refuses to trust me,” say you; and in fact I have sometimes heard this said by some: “Unless I swear oaths without number, the man will not trust me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, and for this you may thank yourself, because you are so off-hand with your oaths.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For were it not so, but on the contrary were it clear to all men that you do not swear, take my word for it, you would be more readily believed upon your mere nod, than those are who swallow oaths by thousands.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For look now: which do you more readily believe? me who do not swear, or those that do swear?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yes,” say you, “but then you are ruler and bishop.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then suppose I prove to you that it is not only for that reason?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Answer me with truth, I beseech you; were I in the habit of perpetually swearing, would my office stand me in that stead?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see that it is not for this reason?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what do you gain at all?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Answer me that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul endured hunger; do you then also choose to hunger rather than to transgress one of the commandments of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why are you so unbelieving?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here are you, ready to do and suffer all things for the sake of not swearing: and shall not He reward you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Shall He, Who sustains day by day both takers and breakers of oaths, give you over to hunger, when you have obeyed Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let all men see, that of those who assemble in this Church not one is a swearer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By this also let us become manifest, and not by our creed alone; let us have this mark also to distinguish us both from the Gentiles and from all men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us receive it as a seal from heaven, that we may everywhere be seen to be the King’s own flock.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By our mouth and tongue let us be known, in the first place, just as the barbarians are by theirs: even as those who speak Greek are distinguished from barbarians, so let us be known.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Answer me: the birds which are said to be parrots, how are they known to be parrots? is it not by speaking like men?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then be known by speaking like the Apostles; by speaking like the Angels.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If any one bid you swear tell him, “Christ has spoken, and I do not swear.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is enough to make a way for all virtue to come in.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a gate to religion, a high road leading to the philosophy of piety; a kind of training-school.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things let us observe, that we may obtain also the future blessings, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, to Whom with the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, power and honor, now and ever, world without end.
